title: add --getlogin to podmain login command
body:
For scripting, a command is needed to determine if podman is currently logged into a registry.  If not logged-in, return a non-zero error.  If logged-in, return the username.

When not logged-in to a registry:
```
$ podman login --get-login docker.io
not logged into docker.io
$ echo $?
125
```
When logged-in to a registry:
```
$ podman login docker.io
Username: deuscapturus
Password: *****
Login Succeeded!
$ podman login --get-login docker.io
deuscapturus
$ echo $?
0
```

comments.body: I think this might be better as a `podman image` subcommand - `podman image getlogin`?

> I think this might be better as a `podman image` subcommand - `podman image getlogin`?

I would agree if the login and logout commands were in image.  I strongly believe this command should live at the same level as login and logout.

Can one of the admins verify this patch?
I understand the following commands:
- `bot, add author to whitelist`
- `bot, test pull request`
- `bot, test pull request once`

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@deuscapturus Your commit is missing a signoff - can you add one with `git commit --amend -s` and repush?
Yes I prefer this to be just an option on podman login, then a new command.

Adding this feature to `login` would fork the logic of the login command into two paths.  This suggests that it really should be its own command.  Perhaps login itself should use sub-commands; but it doesn’t.  If docker-cli were implementing this feature I believe they would do it as a new command.

I would think that we wouldn’t want to change the signature of the login command if anything.  Adding new commands is a good, non-breaking way to innovate beyond the docker-cli.

What is the concern of making this a new command?

> Adding this feature to `login` would fork the logic of the login command into two paths. This suggests that it really should be its own command. Perhaps login itself should use sub-commands; but it doesn’t. If docker-cli were implementing this feature I believe they would do it as a new command.

The code of `podman-login` could be extended with a `if c.BoolT("get-login") { ... return nil }` which I consider to be a common pattern; plus moving it before the `len(args)` checks. What docker-cli would hypothetically do in that case is up to decide for an oracle.

> I would think that we wouldn’t want to change the signature of the login command if anything. Adding new commands is a good, non-breaking way to innovate beyond the docker-cli.

It would not break anything but extend the original docker-cli, which is something Podman is doing on purpose. The goal is to be compatible to the docker-cli as much as possible to not break scripts and help user transition, but that doesn't forbid to add new flags, options, etc.

> What is the concern of making this a new command?

IMHO it logically belongs to the login command. If I'd be a new user, I would intuitivelly look for such functionality in the `podman-login` command. Sub-commands are commonly used to namespace the CLI and to keep it clean, but in this specific case adding a flag is sufficient (and doesn't break docker compat).

@vrothberg covered this very well, but I would just like to add that adding commands, I feel is for something more substantial then this.  And I agree that it is far easier to discover the command by looking at the man page or podman login --help then it would be searching through all of the base commands of podman --help.

I am trying hard to limit the number of new base commands and forcing them into higher level sub-commands, which is why we are adding new stuff to podman image and podman container and just mimicking the Docker CLI commands that already exist.
@deuscapturus Are you still working on this?
Yes.  I'll move the functionality to login.

Need to update man page.
Update command completions
Squash your PRs.

Nice addition to Podman, thanks for running with it.
